docs.text: Activity 1 - Edit, Commit, Repeat 🎯 Scope In this activity you will practice forking, cloning, branching, staging, committing, pushing and pull requests. 💡 Scenario You have been hired by KangaCafe as a software developer to update their website. They don’t like the current orange color scheme and want you to experiment with different color options to find one that works best. You must use VSCode with the LivePreview extension to view your changes to the website. It is recommended to use the cheat sheet while doing this activity. 📋 Instructions Using what you have learned about Git and GitHub, complete the following actions: Fork the activity repository https://github.com/Digital-Initiatives-Kangan-Institute/git-activity-tbd2 Clone your forked copy of the repository Create a new branch on your local copy of the repository Switch to the new branch you just created Open the index.html file Locate and change --color-scheme-primary from orange to dodgerblue Locate and change --color-scheme-secondary from darkorange to skyblue Stage , commit , and push your changes to GitHub Locate and change --color-scheme-primary from dodgerblue to purple Locate and change --color-scheme-secondary from skyblue to darkorchid Stage , commit , and push your changes to GitHub Locate and change --color-scheme-primary from purple to limegreen Locate and change --color-scheme-secondary from darkorchid to lime Stage , commit , and push your changes to GitHub Locate and change --color-scheme-primary and --color-scheme-secondary colors to the best fitting color scheme Stage , commit , and push your changes to GitHub Open a pull request , merging the changes from your new branch into the master branch
docs.title: Activity 1 - Edit, Commit, Repeat

docs.text: Branching Strategy Guide When working on a team or even solo, it’s helpful to organize your code with branches so that development is safe, changes are tested, and releases are controlled. One common strategy uses three main branches : Master (or Main) Branch Staging Branch Dev Branch 1. Master Branch Purpose: The “production-ready” branch. Only fully tested and stable code goes here. Usage: No direct development on this branch. Only merge code that has passed testing in Staging. 2. Staging Branch Purpose: A testing branch that acts as a “preview” before code goes live. Usage: Merge completed features from Dev here for testing. QA or testing is performed on this branch. 3. Dev Branch Purpose: The main branch for active development. Usage: Developers create feature branches from Dev. Merge completed features back into Dev when ready. 4. Feature Branches Purpose: Isolate individual features or bug fixes. Usage: Created from Dev. Merged back into Dev when complete. Recommended Workflow Create feature branches from Dev. Develop and commit in feature branches. Merge feature branches into Dev when done. Test Dev branch , then merge Dev into Staging for broader testing. Test Staging , then merge Staging into Master for release. Benefits of This Strategy Keeps production stable. Encourages testing before release. Allows multiple developers to work in parallel safely. Clear separation of development, testing, and production code.
docs.title: Branching Strategy

docs.text: Installing Git 🪟 Windows 1. Instructions Open your web browser. Go to the official Git website: https://git-scm.com Click Download for Windows . Run the downloaded installer. Follow the installer steps and leave the default options selected. Click Install . 2. Check the installation Open Command Prompt or PowerShell and run: git --version If Git is installed correctly, it will display the installed version. 🐧 Linux 1. Instructions Git can be installed using your distribution’s package manager . Debian / Ubuntu (APT) sudo apt update sudo apt install git Fedora / RHEL (DNF) sudo dnf install git Arch Linux / Manjaro (Pacman) sudo pacman -S git 2. Check the installation git --version 🍎 macOS 1. Instructions The easiest way is to install git is using Homebrew . Install Homebrew from the official website: https://brew.sh/ Run the following command in a terminal: brew install git 2. Check the installation git --version
docs.title: Installing Git

docs.text: Introduction What is Git? Git is a version control system that helps you track changes to your code over time. It allows you to save snapshots of your work so you can return to an earlier version if needed. It also makes collaboration easier by organising and combining changes from different people. Installing Git ... Git vs GitHub ...
docs.title: Introduction

docs.text: Git is a version control system that helps you track changes to your code over time. It allows you to save snapshots of your work so you can return to an earlier version if needed. It also makes collaboration easier by organising and combining changes from different people.
docs.title: What is Git?

docs.text: Pull Requests Reviewing When merging pull requests into your repository, it is important to review the proposed changes before accepting them. A pull request review helps ensure that the code being added is correct, safe, and follows the standards of the project. It also helps prevent mistakes from being introduced into the main codebase. When reviewing a pull request, you should check the following: The changes match what is intended Review the files to ensure the pull request only contains the changes needed for the task and no accidental or unrelated edits. The changes are targeting the correct branch Check that the pull request is being merged into the correct branch (such as main or develop) to avoid disrupting the workflow. The code meets team quality standards Ensure the code follows the team’s coding standards, is readable, and avoids unnecessary complexity. The changes do not introduce bugs Consider whether the update could break existing functionality and confirm the project still works as expected. Documentation or comments are included if needed Make sure any complex logic or new features are explained so other developers can understand and maintain the code. Taking a few minutes to properly review a pull request helps maintain code quality, stability, and collaboration within the team. It also provides an opportunity to give constructive feedback and help other developers improve their work.
docs.title: Pull Requests

docs.text: Git Cheatsheet 1. git branch Purpose: Lists all branches or creates a new branch. Example: # List all branches git branch # Create a new branch called 'feature-login' git branch feature-login 2. git switch Purpose: Switches to another branch. Example: # Switch to an existing branch git switch main # Switch to a newly created branch git switch feature-login 3. git add Purpose: Stages changes in your working directory for the next commit. Example: # Stage a single file git add index.html # Stage all changes git add . 4. git pull Purpose: Fetches and merges changes from a remote repository into your current branch. Example: # Pull changes from the remote 'main' branch git pull origin main 5. git push Purpose: Sends your committed changes to a remote repository. Example: # Push changes to the remote 'main' branch git push origin main 6. git status Purpose: Shows the status of changes in your working directory and staging area. Example: # Check which files are staged, modified, or untracked git status 7. git restore Purpose: Discards changes in the working directory or restores files from the staging area. Example: # Discard changes in a single file git restore index.html # Unstage a file git restore --staged index.html 8. git clone Purpose: Creates a local copy of a remote repository. Example: # Clone a repository from GitHub git clone https://github.com/username/repo.git
docs.title: Git Cheatsheet
